---
title: "MAT 142: Logarithmic Functions"
author: Dr. Gilbert
format: 
  revealjs:
    smaller: true
date: today
date-format: long
theme: serif
incremental: true
---

```{r global-options, include=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)

theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 16))
```

```{css}
code.sourceCode {
  font-size: 1.3em;
  /* or try font-size: xx-large; */
}

a {
  color: purple;
}

a:link {
  color: purple;
}

a:visited {
  color: purple;
}
```

## Reminders

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

Last class we investigated exponential functions — their geography, transformations, inverses, and applications to compound interest and decay. Try the following warm-up problems.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 1:** Solve $\displaystyle{\log_5\!\left(x + 10\right) = 30}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 2:** Solve $\displaystyle{\ln\!\left(x + 5\right) = 8 - \ln\!\left(x + 1\right)}$. Check your solution carefully.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Note:* Problem 2 requires combining logarithms before exponentiating. The resulting equation is quadratic — and one solution will be extraneous.

:::

</div>

## Objectives

:::{.fragment}

Since logarithmic and exponential functions are inverses of each other, much of today's analysis mirrors last class — but with the roles of domain/range, asymptotes, and intercepts swapped.

:::

:::{.fragment}

After today's class meeting, you should be able to:

:::

+ Identify and sketch the graph of the logarithmic book function $f\left(x\right) = \log_a\!\left(x\right)$.
+ Describe the domain, range, asymptote, and end behavior of a logarithmic function.
+ Apply transformations to sketch graphs of logarithmic functions.
+ Find the inverse of a logarithmic function.
+ Apply exponential and logarithmic models to real-world problems.

## The Logarithmic Book Function

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Definition:** The *logarithmic book function* is $f\left(x\right) = \log_a\!\left(x\right)$, where $a > 0$ and $a \neq 1$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

Since $f\left(x\right) = \log_a\!\left(x\right)$ is the inverse of $g\left(x\right) = a^x$, its graph is the reflection of the exponential graph across the line $y = x$. Every feature swaps:

:::

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

| Exponential $a^x$ | Logarithmic $\log_a\!\left(x\right)$ |
|:--|:--|
| Domain: $\left(-\infty, \infty\right)$ | Domain: $\left(0, \infty\right)$ |
| Range: $\left(0, \infty\right)$ | Range: $\left(-\infty, \infty\right)$ |
| $y$-intercept: $\left(0, 1\right)$ | $x$-intercept: $\left(1, 0\right)$ |
| Horizontal asymptote: $y = 0$ | Vertical asymptote: $x = 0$ |

:::

:::{.fragment}

The behavior again depends on the base:

:::

+ $a > 1$: **increasing** — the logarithm grows without bound, but slowly.
+ $0 < a < 1$: **decreasing**.

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

```{r}
x_exp_vals <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
y_exp_vals <- exp(x_exp_vals)

x_log_vals <- seq(0.005, 5, length.out = 500)
y_log_vals <- log(x_log_vals)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_exp_vals, y = y_exp_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_log_vals, y = y_log_vals), color = "blue") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = -2, xend = 5, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -5, yend = 5,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-2, 5), ylim = c(-5, 5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-2, 5, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = \\log_a(x)$, $a > 1$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

:::{.fragment}

```{r}
x_exp_vals <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
y_exp_vals <- 0.5^(x_exp_vals)

x_log_vals <- seq(0.005, 5, length.out = 500)
y_log_vals <- log(x_log_vals)/log(0.5)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_exp_vals, y = y_exp_vals), color = "darkred", linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_log_vals, y = y_log_vals), color = "darkred") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = -2, xend = 5, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -5, yend = 5,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-2, 5), ylim = c(-5, 5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-2, 5, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = \\log_a(x)$, $0 < a < 1$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## The Logarithmic Book Function

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Definition:** The *logarithmic book function* is $f\left(x\right) = \log_a\!\left(x\right)$, where $a > 0$ and $a \neq 1$.

Since $f\left(x\right) = \log_a\!\left(x\right)$ is the inverse of $g\left(x\right) = a^x$, its graph is the reflection of the exponential graph across the line $y = x$. Every feature swaps:

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

| Exponential $a^x$ | Logarithmic $\log_a\!\left(x\right)$ |
|:--|:--|
| Domain: $\left(-\infty, \infty\right)$ | Domain: $\left(0, \infty\right)$ |
| Range: $\left(0, \infty\right)$ | Range: $\left(-\infty, \infty\right)$ |
| $y$-intercept: $\left(0, 1\right)$ | $x$-intercept: $\left(1, 0\right)$ |
| Horizontal asymptote: $y = 0$ | Vertical asymptote: $x = 0$ |

The behavior again depends on the base:

:::{.nonincremental}

+ $a > 1$: **increasing** — the logarithm grows without bound, but slowly.
+ $0 < a < 1$: **decreasing**.

:::

The faded curves in the plots on the right are the corresponding exponentials.

:::{.fragment}

The dashed line is the $y = x$ line, allowing us to see that the graph of the logarithmic functions are reflections of the graphs of the exponentials.

:::

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

```{r}
x_exp_vals <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
y_exp_vals <- exp(x_exp_vals)

x_log_vals <- seq(0.005, 5, length.out = 500)
y_log_vals <- log(x_log_vals)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_exp_vals, y = y_exp_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_log_vals, y = y_log_vals), color = "blue") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = -2, xend = 5, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -5, yend = 5,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-2, 5), ylim = c(-5, 5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-2, 5, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = \\log_a(x)$, $a > 1$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

```{r}
x_exp_vals <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
y_exp_vals <- 0.5^(x_exp_vals)

x_log_vals <- seq(0.005, 5, length.out = 500)
y_log_vals <- log(x_log_vals)/log(0.5)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_exp_vals, y = y_exp_vals), color = "darkred", linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_log_vals, y = y_log_vals), color = "darkred") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = -2, xend = 5, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -5, yend = 5,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-2, 5), ylim = c(-5, 5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-2, 5, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = \\log_a(x)$, $0 < a < 1$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

::::

</div>

## Transformations of Logarithmic Functions

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

For $f\left(x\right) = A\cdot\log_a\!\left(B\left(x - H\right)\right) + K$, the key effects are:

:::

+ The **vertical asymptote** shifts from $x = 0$ to $x = H$.
+ The **domain** shifts from $\left(0, \infty\right)$ to $\left(H, \infty\right)$ if $B > 0$ or to $\left(-\infty, H\right)$ if $B < 0$.
+ The **$x$-intercept** changes. Solve $f\left(x\right) = 0$ to find it.
+ The **$y$-intercept** may or may not exist. If $x = 0$ is in the domain of the function, evaluate $f\left(0\right)$ to find the $y$-intercept.
+ End behavior adjusts accordingly.


</div>

:::{.fragment}

:::{.callout-note}
## The Mirror Image

Since $\log_a$ and $a^x$ are inverses, the logarithm's vertical asymptote plays the same role as the exponential's horizontal asymptote — it's the boundary the function approaches but never crosses. The logarithm approaches it as $x$ approaches the asymptote from the side on which the function values are defined; the exponential approaches its asymptote as $x \to \pm\infty$.

:::

:::

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

:::

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Domain:**

:::

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** The argument of the logarithm ($x - 5$) must be positive.

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} x - 5 > 0
\end{align}

:::

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** The argument of the logarithm ($x - 5$) must be positive.

\begin{align} x - 5 > 0\\
x > 5
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

:::{.fragment}

**Vertical Asymptote:**

:::

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

**Vertical Asymptote:** Since the graph was shifted $5$ to the right, the asymptote is shifted that same way.

:::{.fragment}

The asymptote for the comparison/base function was at $x = 0$

:::

:::{.fragment}

The asymptote for the translated function is at $x = 5$

:::

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

**Vertical Asymptote:** $x = 5$

:::{.fragment}

**$y$-intercept:**

:::

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

**Vertical Asymptote:** $x = 5$

**$y$-intercept:** $x = 0$ is not in the domain of $f\left(x\right)$, so there is no $y$ intercept.

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

**Vertical Asymptote:** $x = 5$

**$y$-intercept:** None

:::{.fragment}

**$x$-intercept:**

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9 = 0
\end{align}

:::

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

**Vertical Asymptote:** $x = 5$

**$y$-intercept:** None

**$x$-intercept:**

\begin{align} \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9 = 0\\
\implies \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 9
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

**Vertical Asymptote:** $x = 5$

**$y$-intercept:** None

**$x$-intercept:**

\begin{align} \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9 = 0\\
\implies \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 9\\
\implies \ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 6
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

**Vertical Asymptote:** $x = 5$

**$y$-intercept:** None

**$x$-intercept:**

\begin{align} \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9 = 0\\
\implies \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 9\\
\implies \ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 6\\
\implies x - 5 &= e^6
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

**Vertical Asymptote:** $x = 5$

**$y$-intercept:** None

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(5 + e^6, 0\right)}$

\begin{align} \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9 = 0\\
\implies \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 9\\
\implies \ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 6\\
\implies x - 5 &= e^6
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::{.fragment}

**End behavior:**

:::

:::{.fragment}

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to -\infty}{\frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{\frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9}}$

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

**Vertical Asymptote:** $x = 5$

**$y$-intercept:** None

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(5 + e^6, 0\right)}$

\begin{align} \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9 = 0\\
\implies \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 9\\
\implies \ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 6\\
\implies x - 5 &= e^6
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**End behavior:**

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to 5^+}{\frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9}} = -\infty$

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{\frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9}}$

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 1:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = \frac{3}{2}\ln\!\left(x - 5\right) - 9}$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* Base function is $\ln\!\left(x\right)$, but it has been...

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$ 
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $\frac{3}{2}$
+ shifted *downward* by $9$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > 5$ or $\left(5, \infty\right)$

**Vertical Asymptote:** $x = 5$

**$y$-intercept:** None

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(5 + e^6, 0\right)}$

\begin{align} \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9 = 0\\
\implies \frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 9\\
\implies \ln\left(x - 5\right) &= 6\\
\implies x - 5 &= e^6
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**End behavior:**

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to 5^+}{\frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9} = -\infty}$

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{\frac{3}{2}\ln\left(x - 5\right) - 9} = \infty}$

:::{.fragment}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| fig.height: 3.5

x_vals <- seq(5.01, 20, length.out = 500)
f_vals <- (3/2)*log(x_vals - 5) - 9

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = f_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 5, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(x = exp(6) + 5, y = 0), size = 3, color = "black") +
  annotate("text", x = 6, y = 2, label = "x = 5", color = "gray40", size = 6) +
  annotate("segment", x = -1, xend = 20, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -12, yend = 3,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-1, 20), ylim = c(-12, 3)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-1, 20, by = 3)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = \\frac{3}{2}\\ln(x-5) - 9$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::


:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

:::

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Domain:** 

:::

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** The argument to the logarithm must be positive.

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} x + 4 &> 0
\end{align}

:::


:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** The argument to the logarithm must be positive.

:::{.fragmet}

\begin{align} x + 4 &> 0\\
x &> -4
\end{align}

:::


:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

\begin{align} x + 4 &> 0\\
x &> -4
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

:::{.fragment}

**Vertical asymptote:** 

:::

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** Since the graph is shifted *left* by $4$, so is the vertical asymptote.

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

:::{.fragment}

**$y$-intercept:** 

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} g\left(0\right) &= -8\log_{2}\left(0 + 4\right) + 6
\end{align}

:::

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** 

\begin{align} g\left(0\right) &= -8\log_{2}\left(0 + 4\right) + 6\\
&= -8\log_{2}\left(4\right) + 6
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** 

\begin{align} g\left(0\right) &= -8\log_{2}\left(0 + 4\right) + 6\\
&= -8\log_{2}\left(4\right) + 6\\
&= -8\left(2\right) + 6
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** 

\begin{align} g\left(0\right) &= -8\log_{2}\left(0 + 4\right) + 6\\
&= -8\log_{2}\left(4\right) + 6\\
&= -8\left(2\right) + 6\\
&= -10
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

\begin{align} g\left(0\right) &= -8\log_{2}\left(0 + 4\right) + 6\\
&= -8\log_{2}\left(4\right) + 6\\
&= -8\left(2\right) + 6\\
&= -10
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

:::{.fragment}

**$x$-intercept:** 

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6 &= 0
\end{align}

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** 

\begin{align} -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6 &= 0\\
\implies -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= -6
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** 

\begin{align} -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6 &= 0\\
\implies -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= -6\\
\implies \log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= \frac{-6}{-8}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** 

\begin{align} -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6 &= 0\\
\implies -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= -6\\
\implies \log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= \frac{3}{4}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** 

\begin{align} -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6 &= 0\\
\implies -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= -6\\
\implies \log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= \frac{3}{4}\\
\implies 2^{\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right)} &= 2^{3/4}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** 

\begin{align} -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6 &= 0\\
\implies -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= -6\\
\implies \log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= \frac{3}{4}\\
\implies 2^{\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right)} &= 2^{3/4}\\
\implies x + 4 &= 8^{1/4}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** 

\begin{align} -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6 &= 0\\
\implies -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= -6\\
\implies \log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= \frac{3}{4}\\
\implies 2^{\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right)} &= 2^{3/4}\\
\implies x + 4 &= 8^{1/4}\\
\implies x &= 8^{1/4} - 4
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(8^{1/4} - 4, 0\right)}$

\begin{align} -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6 &= 0\\
\implies -8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= -6\\
\implies \log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) &= \frac{3}{4}\\
\implies 2^{\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right)} &= 2^{3/4}\\
\implies x + 4 &= 8^{1/4}\\
\implies x &= 8^{1/4} - 4
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(8^{1/4} - 4, 0\right)}$


:::{.fragment}

**End behavior:**

:::

:::{.fragment}

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to -4^+}{-8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to \infty}{-8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6}}$

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(8^{1/4} - 4, 0\right)}$

**End behavior:**

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to -4^+}{-8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6} = \infty}$

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to \infty}{-8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6}}$

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(8^{1/4} - 4, 0\right)}$

**End behavior:**

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to -4^+}{-8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6} = \infty}$

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to \infty}{-8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6} = -\infty}$

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(8^{1/4} - 4, 0\right)}$

**End behavior:** $\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to -4^+}{g\left(x\right)} = \infty}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to \infty}{g\left(x\right)} = -\infty}$

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to -4^+}{-8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6} = \infty}$

$\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to \infty}{-8\log_{2}\left(x + 4\right) + 6} = -\infty}$

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -8\log_2\!\left(x + 4\right) + 6$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept, $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch a graph.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="45%"}

*Solution.* The base/comparison function is $\log_2\left(x\right)$, but... 

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $4$ 
+ *reflected* across the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* vertically by a factor of $8$ 
+ shifted *upwards* by $6$.

:::

**Domain:** $x > -4$ or $\left(-4, \infty\right)$

**Vertical asymptote:** $x = -4$

**$y$-intercept:** $\left(0, -10\right)$

:::

:::{.column width="55%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(8^{1/4} - 4, 0\right)}$

**End behavior:** $\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to -4^+}{g\left(x\right)} = \infty}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x \to \infty}{g\left(x\right)} = -\infty}$

:::{.fragment}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| fig.height: 3.25

x_vals <- seq(-3.99, 10, length.out = 500)
g_vals <- -8*log(x_vals + 4)/log(2) + 6

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = g_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -4, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 0, y = -10), size = 3, color = "black") +
  geom_point(aes(x = 2^(3/4) - 4, y = 0), size = 3, color = "black") +
  annotate("text", x = -3.5, y = 8, label = "x = -4", color = "gray40", size = 4) +
  annotate("segment", x = -4, xend = 10, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -15, yend = 10,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-4, 10), ylim = c(-15, 10)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-4, 10, by = 2)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$g(x) = -8\\log_2(x+4) + 6$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Logarithmic Function Practice

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Try It! 1:** For $f\left(x\right) = \log_3\!\left(x + 2\right) - 4$, identify the domain, vertical asymptote, $y$-intercept (if it exists), $x$-intercept, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

<br/>
<br/>
<br/>
<br/>

**Try It! 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -2\ln\!\left(x - 1\right) + 3$, identify the same features and sketch a graph.

*Before sketching:* The negative leading coefficient flips the graph. How does that affect the end behavior and range compared to the basic $\ln\!\left(x\right)$ function?

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

Since logarithmic functions are one-to-one, they are invertible. Their inverses are exponential functions.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Strategy:** Swap-and-solve, using exponentiation to undo the logarithm once it has been isolated.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $f\left(x\right) = \log\!\left(4x - 5\right) + 8$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* Recall $\log$ without a base denotes $\log_{10}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} y &= \log_{10}\!\left(4x - 5\right) + 8
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Since logarithmic functions are one-to-one, they are invertible. Their inverses are exponential functions.

**Strategy:** Swap-and-solve, using exponentiation to undo the logarithm once it has been isolated.

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $f\left(x\right) = \log\left(4x - 5\right) + 8$.

*Solution.* Recall $\log$ without a base denotes $\log_{10}$.

\begin{align} y &= \log_{10}\!\left(4x - 5\right) + 8\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Since logarithmic functions are one-to-one, they are invertible. Their inverses are exponential functions.

**Strategy:** Swap-and-solve, using exponentiation to undo the logarithm once it has been isolated.

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $f\left(x\right) = \log\left(4x - 5\right) + 8$.

*Solution.* Recall $\log$ without a base denotes $\log_{10}$.

\begin{align} y &= \log_{10}\!\left(4x - 5\right) + 8\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8 &= x
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Since logarithmic functions are one-to-one, they are invertible. Their inverses are exponential functions.

**Strategy:** Swap-and-solve, using exponentiation to undo the logarithm once it has been isolated.

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $f\left(x\right) = \log\left(4x - 5\right) + 8$.

*Solution.* Recall $\log$ without a base denotes $\log_{10}$.

\begin{align} y &= \log_{10}\!\left(4x - 5\right) + 8\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8 &= x\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) &= x - 8
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Since logarithmic functions are one-to-one, they are invertible. Their inverses are exponential functions.

**Strategy:** Swap-and-solve, using exponentiation to undo the logarithm once it has been isolated.

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $f\left(x\right) = \log\left(4x - 5\right) + 8$.

*Solution.* Recall $\log$ without a base denotes $\log_{10}$.

\begin{align} y &= \log_{10}\!\left(4x - 5\right) + 8\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8 &= x\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) &= x - 8\\
\implies 10^{\log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right)} &= 10^{x - 8}
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Since logarithmic functions are one-to-one, they are invertible. Their inverses are exponential functions.

**Strategy:** Swap-and-solve, using exponentiation to undo the logarithm once it has been isolated.

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $f\left(x\right) = \log\left(4x - 5\right) + 8$.

*Solution.* Recall $\log$ without a base denotes $\log_{10}$.

\begin{align} y &= \log_{10}\!\left(4x - 5\right) + 8\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8 &= x\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) &= x - 8\\
\implies 10^{\log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right)} &= 10^{x - 8}\\
\implies 4y - 5 &= 10^{x - 8}
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Since logarithmic functions are one-to-one, they are invertible. Their inverses are exponential functions.

**Strategy:** Swap-and-solve, using exponentiation to undo the logarithm once it has been isolated.

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $f\left(x\right) = \log\left(4x - 5\right) + 8$.

*Solution.* Recall $\log$ without a base denotes $\log_{10}$.

\begin{align} y &= \log_{10}\!\left(4x - 5\right) + 8\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8 &= x\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) &= x - 8\\
\implies 10^{\log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right)} &= 10^{x - 8}\\
\implies 4y - 5 &= 10^{x - 8}\\
\implies 4y &= 10^{x - 8} + 5
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Since logarithmic functions are one-to-one, they are invertible. Their inverses are exponential functions.

**Strategy:** Swap-and-solve, using exponentiation to undo the logarithm once it has been isolated.

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $f\left(x\right) = \log\left(4x - 5\right) + 8$.

*Solution.* Recall $\log$ without a base denotes $\log_{10}$.

\begin{align} y &= \log_{10}\!\left(4x - 5\right) + 8\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) + 8 &= x\\
\implies \log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right) &= x - 8\\
\implies 10^{\log_{10}\left(4y - 5\right)} &= 10^{x - 8}\\
\implies 4y - 5 &= 10^{x - 8}\\
\implies 4y &= 10^{x - 8} + 5\\
\implies y &= \frac{10^{x - 8} + 5}{4}
\end{align}

:::{.fragment}

So $\boxed{~\displaystyle{f^{-1}\left(x\right) = \frac{10^{x - 8} + 5}{4}}~}$.

:::

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \frac{5 - \ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2} + 1}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* Simplify first: $g\left(x\right) = \dfrac{7}{2} - \dfrac{\ln\!\left(x-2\right)}{2}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} y &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2}
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \frac{5 - \ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2} + 1}$.

*Solution.* Simplify first: $g\left(x\right) = \dfrac{7}{2} - \dfrac{\ln\!\left(x-2\right)}{2}$.
\begin{align} y &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2}\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2}
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \frac{5 - \ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2} + 1}$.

*Solution.* Simplify first: $g\left(x\right) = \dfrac{7}{2} - \dfrac{\ln\!\left(x-2\right)}{2}$.
\begin{align} y &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2}\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2}\\
\implies \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2} &= x
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \frac{5 - \ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2} + 1}$.

*Solution.* Simplify first: $g\left(x\right) = \dfrac{7}{2} - \dfrac{\ln\!\left(x-2\right)}{2}$.
\begin{align} y &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2}\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2}\\
\implies \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2} &= x\\
\implies 7 - \ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \frac{5 - \ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2} + 1}$.

*Solution.* Simplify first: $g\left(x\right) = \dfrac{7}{2} - \dfrac{\ln\!\left(x-2\right)}{2}$.
\begin{align} y &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2}\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2}\\
\implies \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2} &= x\\
\implies 7 - \ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x\\
\implies -\ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x -7
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \frac{5 - \ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2} + 1}$.

*Solution.* Simplify first: $g\left(x\right) = \dfrac{7}{2} - \dfrac{\ln\!\left(x-2\right)}{2}$.
\begin{align} y &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2}\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2}\\
\implies \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2} &= x\\
\implies 7 - \ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x\\
\implies -\ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x -7\\
\implies \ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 7 - 2x
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \frac{5 - \ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2} + 1}$.

*Solution.* Simplify first: $g\left(x\right) = \dfrac{7}{2} - \dfrac{\ln\!\left(x-2\right)}{2}$.
\begin{align} y &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2}\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2}\\
\implies \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2} &= x\\
\implies 7 - \ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x\\
\implies -\ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x -7\\
\implies \ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 7 - 2x\\
\implies e^{\ln\left(y - 2\right)} &= e^{7 - 2x}
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \frac{5 - \ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2} + 1}$.

*Solution.* Simplify first: $g\left(x\right) = \dfrac{7}{2} - \dfrac{\ln\!\left(x-2\right)}{2}$.
\begin{align} y &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2}\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2}\\
\implies \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2} &= x\\
\implies 7 - \ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x\\
\implies -\ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x -7\\
\implies \ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 7 - 2x\\
\implies e^{\ln\left(y - 2\right)} &= e^{7 - 2x}\\
\implies y - 2 &= e^{7 - 2x}
\end{align}

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = \frac{5 - \ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2} + 1}$.

*Solution.* Simplify first: $g\left(x\right) = \dfrac{7}{2} - \dfrac{\ln\!\left(x-2\right)}{2}$.
\begin{align} y &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\!\left(x - 2\right)}{2}\\
\stackrel{\text{Swap}}{} x &= \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2}\\
\implies \frac{7}{2} - \frac{\ln\left(y - 2\right)}{2} &= x\\
\implies 7 - \ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x\\
\implies -\ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 2x -7\\
\implies \ln\left(y - 2\right) &= 7 - 2x\\
\implies e^{\ln\left(y - 2\right)} &= e^{7 - 2x}\\
\implies y - 2 &= e^{7 - 2x}\\
\implies y &= e^{7 - 2x} + 2
\end{align}

:::{.fragment}

So, $\boxed{~\displaystyle{g^{-1}\left(x\right) = e^{7 - 2x} + 2}~}$.

:::

</div>

## Inverses of Logarithmic Functions Practice

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Try It! 3:** Find the inverse of $f\left(x\right) = \log_3\!\left(x + 2\right) - 4$ from Try It! 1. State the domain of $f^{-1}$.

<br/>
<br/>
<br/>
<br/>

**Try It! 4:** Find the inverse of $g\left(x\right) = -2\ln\!\left(x - 1\right) + 3$ from Try It! 2. State the domain of $g^{-1}$.

</div>

## Exponential Growth and Decay

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

Many real-world quantities change at a rate proportional to their current size. This produces the model:

$$A = Pe^{rt}$$

where $P$ is the initial quantity, $r$ is the continuous rate of growth ($r > 0$) or decay ($r < 0$), $t$ is time, and $A$ is the quantity at time $t$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

This is the same continuous compounding formula from last class — extended now as a general model for any exponential process.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Key insight:** Solving for $t$ always requires a logarithm, since $t$ is in the exponent. This is one reason why logarithms are almost always introduced immediately following exponential functions.

:::

</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

:::

:::{.fragment}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}
\end{align}

:::

::::

:::


</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= e^{r\cdot 5730}
\end{align}

:::

::::


</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= e^{r\cdot 5730}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= r\cdot 5730
\end{align}

:::

::::


</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= e^{r\cdot 5730}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= r\cdot 5730\\
\implies r &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{5730}
\end{align}

:::

::::


</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= e^{r\cdot 5730}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= r\cdot 5730\\
\implies r &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{5730}\\
\implies r &= \frac{-\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

Now, solve for $t$ using the discovered decay rate, the initial amount of Carbon-14 and the current amount of Carbon-14.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} 12 &= 32e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}
\end{align}

:::

:::

::::


</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= e^{r\cdot 5730}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= r\cdot 5730\\
\implies r &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{5730}\\
\implies r &= \frac{-\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

Now, solve for $t$ using the discovered decay rate, the initial amount of Carbon-14 and the current amount of Carbon-14.

\begin{align} 12 &= 32e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}\\
\implies \frac{12}{32} &= e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= e^{r\cdot 5730}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= r\cdot 5730\\
\implies r &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{5730}\\
\implies r &= \frac{-\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

Now, solve for $t$ using the discovered decay rate, the initial amount of Carbon-14 and the current amount of Carbon-14.

\begin{align} 12 &= 32e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}\\
\implies \frac{3}{8} &= e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= e^{r\cdot 5730}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= r\cdot 5730\\
\implies r &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{5730}\\
\implies r &= \frac{-\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

Now, solve for $t$ using the discovered decay rate, the initial amount of Carbon-14 and the current amount of Carbon-14.

\begin{align} 12 &= 32e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}\\
\implies \frac{3}{8} &= e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{3}{8}\right) &= -\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= e^{r\cdot 5730}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= r\cdot 5730\\
\implies r &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{5730}\\
\implies r &= \frac{-\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

Now, solve for $t$ using the discovered decay rate, the initial amount of Carbon-14 and the current amount of Carbon-14.

\begin{align} 12 &= 32e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}\\
\implies \frac{3}{8} &= e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{3}{8}\right) &= -\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t\\
\implies -\ln\left(2\right)t &= 5730\ln\left(\frac{3}{8}\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= e^{r\cdot 5730}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= r\cdot 5730\\
\implies r &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{5730}\\
\implies r &= \frac{-\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

Now, solve for $t$ using the discovered decay rate, the initial amount of Carbon-14 and the current amount of Carbon-14.

\begin{align} 12 &= 32e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}\\
\implies \frac{3}{8} &= e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{3}{8}\right) &= -\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t\\
\implies -\ln\left(2\right)t &= 5730\ln\left(\frac{3}{8}\right)\\
\implies t &= -\frac{5730\ln\left(3/8\right)}{\ln\left(2\right)}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Carbon Dating

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 5:** The half-life of Carbon-14 is 5730 years. A fossil contains 12 grams of C-14. If we know it originally contained 32 grams, how old is the fossil?

*Solution.* Using the decay model $A = Pe^{rt}$, first find $r$ from the half-life condition that after 5730 years, half remains.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

\begin{align} \frac{P}{2} &= Pe^{r \cdot 5730}\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= e^{r\cdot 5730}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= r\cdot 5730\\
\implies r &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{5730}\\
\implies r &= \frac{-\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

Now, solve for $t$ using the discovered decay rate, the initial amount of Carbon-14 and the current amount of Carbon-14.

\begin{align} 12 &= 32e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}\\
\implies \frac{3}{8} &= e^{-\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t}\\
\implies \ln\left(\frac{3}{8}\right) &= -\frac{\ln\left(2\right)}{5730}t\\
\implies -\ln\left(2\right)t &= 5730\ln\left(\frac{3}{8}\right)\\
\implies t &= -\frac{5730\ln\left(3/8\right)}{\ln\left(2\right)}\\
\implies t &\approx 8108
\end{align}

:::

:::{.fragment}

The fossil is approximately 8,108 years old.

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Drug Dosages

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Example 6:** A doctor prescribes 125 mg of a drug that decays by about 30% each hour.

$\left(a\right)$ To the nearest hour, what is the half-life of the drug?

$\left(b\right)$ Write an exponential model and find the amount remaining after 3 hours.

$\left(c\right)$ Find and interpret $f\left(10\right)$.

:::

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

**(a)** The model is $A = 125\left(0.7\right)^t$ since the concentration decays hourly.

:::

:::{.fragment}

At the time of one half-life, there will be 62.5mg of the drug remaining.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} 62.5 &= 125\left(0.7\right)^t
\end{align}

:::

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Drug Dosages

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** A doctor prescribes 125 mg of a drug that decays by about 30% each hour.

$\left(a\right)$ To the nearest hour, what is the half-life of the drug?

$\left(b\right)$ Write an exponential model and find the amount remaining after 3 hours.

$\left(c\right)$ Find and interpret $f\left(10\right)$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)** The model is $A = 125\left(0.7\right)^t$ since the concentration decays hourly.

At the time of one half-life, there will be 62.5mg of the drug remaining.

\begin{align} 62.5 &= 125\left(0.7\right)^t\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= 0.7^t
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Drug Dosages

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** A doctor prescribes 125 mg of a drug that decays by about 30% each hour.

$\left(a\right)$ To the nearest hour, what is the half-life of the drug?

$\left(b\right)$ Write an exponential model and find the amount remaining after 3 hours.

$\left(c\right)$ Find and interpret $f\left(10\right)$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)** The model is $A = 125\left(0.7\right)^t$ since the concentration decays hourly.

At the time of one half-life, there will be 62.5mg of the drug remaining.

\begin{align} 62.5 &= 125\left(0.7\right)^t\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= 0.7^t\\
\implies t\ln\left(0.7\right) &= \ln\left(0.5\right)
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Drug Dosages

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** A doctor prescribes 125 mg of a drug that decays by about 30% each hour.

$\left(a\right)$ To the nearest hour, what is the half-life of the drug?

$\left(b\right)$ Write an exponential model and find the amount remaining after 3 hours.

$\left(c\right)$ Find and interpret $f\left(10\right)$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)** The model is $A = 125\left(0.7\right)^t$ since the concentration decays hourly.

At the time of one half-life, there will be 62.5mg of the drug remaining.

\begin{align} 62.5 &= 125\left(0.7\right)^t\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= 0.7^t\\
\implies t\ln\left(0.7\right) &= \ln\left(0.5\right)\\
\implies t &= \frac{\ln\left(0.5\right)}{\ln\left(0.7\right)}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Drug Dosages

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** A doctor prescribes 125 mg of a drug that decays by about 30% each hour.

$\left(a\right)$ To the nearest hour, what is the half-life of the drug?

$\left(b\right)$ Write an exponential model and find the amount remaining after 3 hours.

$\left(c\right)$ Find and interpret $f\left(10\right)$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)** The model is $A = 125\left(0.7\right)^t$ since the concentration decays hourly.

At the time of one half-life, there will be 62.5mg of the drug remaining.

\begin{align} 62.5 &= 125\left(0.7\right)^t\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= 0.7^t\\
\implies t\ln\left(0.7\right) &= \ln\left(0.5\right)\\
\implies t &= \frac{\ln\left(0.5\right)}{\ln\left(0.7\right)}\\
\implies t &\approx 1.94 \approx 2~\text{hours}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Drug Dosages

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 6:** A doctor prescribes 125 mg of a drug that decays by about 30% each hour.

$\left(a\right)$ To the nearest hour, what is the half-life of the drug?

$\left(b\right)$ Write an exponential model and find the amount remaining after 3 hours.

$\left(c\right)$ Find and interpret $f\left(10\right)$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)** The model is $A = 125\left(0.7\right)^t$ since the concentration decays hourly.

At the time of one half-life, there will be 62.5mg of the drug remaining.

\begin{align} 62.5 &= 125\left(0.7\right)^t\\
\implies \frac{1}{2} &= 0.7^t\\
\implies t\ln\left(0.7\right) &= \ln\left(0.5\right)\\
\implies t &= \frac{\ln\left(0.5\right)}{\ln\left(0.7\right)}\\
\implies t &\approx 1.94 \approx 2~\text{hours}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

**(b)** $f\left(t\right) = 125\left(0.7\right)^t$. 

:::

:::{.fragment}

After 3 hours:

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} f\left(3\right) &= 125\left(0.7\right)^3 = 125\left(0.343\right) \approx 43 \text{ mg}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.fragment}

**(c)**

:::

:::{.fragment}

$\displaystyle{f\left(10\right) = 125\left(0.7\right)^{10} \approx 3.53~\text{mg}}$

:::


:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Earthquake Magnitude

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Example 7:** The Moment-Magnitude Scale (MMS) gives earthquake magnitude as $\displaystyle{M = \frac{2}{3}\log\!\left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)}$, where $E$ is energy released (joules) and $E_0 = 10^{4.4}$ J.

$\left(a\right)$ Find the magnitude of a quake releasing $1.4 \times 10^{13}$ J.

$\left(b\right)$ A second earthquake has 750 times the energy of a magnitude-3.9 quake. Find its magnitude.

:::

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

**(a)**

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} M &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{1.4 \times 10^{13}}{10^{4.4}}\right)
\end{align}

:::

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Earthquake Magnitude

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 7:** The Moment-Magnitude Scale (MMS) gives earthquake magnitude as $\displaystyle{M = \frac{2}{3}\log\!\left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)}$, where $E$ is energy released (joules) and $E_0 = 10^{4.4}$ J.

$\left(a\right)$ Find the magnitude of a quake releasing $1.4 \times 10^{13}$ J.

$\left(b\right)$ A second earthquake has 750 times the energy of a magnitude-3.9 quake. Find its magnitude.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)**

\begin{align} M &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{1.4 \times 10^{13}}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(1.4\times 10^{8.6}\right)
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Earthquake Magnitude

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 7:** The Moment-Magnitude Scale (MMS) gives earthquake magnitude as $\displaystyle{M = \frac{2}{3}\log\!\left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)}$, where $E$ is energy released (joules) and $E_0 = 10^{4.4}$ J.

$\left(a\right)$ Find the magnitude of a quake releasing $1.4 \times 10^{13}$ J.

$\left(b\right)$ A second earthquake has 750 times the energy of a magnitude-3.9 quake. Find its magnitude.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)**

\begin{align} M &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{1.4 \times 10^{13}}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(1.4\times 10^{8.6}\right)\\
&\approx 5.83
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

**(b)** Let $E_1$ be the energy released by the $3.9$ magnitude earthquake.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} M_2 &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{750E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)
\end{align}

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Earthquake Magnitude

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 7:** The Moment-Magnitude Scale (MMS) gives earthquake magnitude as $\displaystyle{M = \frac{2}{3}\log\!\left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)}$, where $E$ is energy released (joules) and $E_0 = 10^{4.4}$ J.

$\left(a\right)$ Find the magnitude of a quake releasing $1.4 \times 10^{13}$ J.

$\left(b\right)$ A second earthquake has 750 times the energy of a magnitude-3.9 quake. Find its magnitude.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)**

\begin{align} M &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{1.4 \times 10^{13}}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(1.4\times 10^{8.6}\right)\\
&\approx 5.83
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(b)** Let $E_1$ be the energy released by the $3.9$ magnitude earthquake.

\begin{align} M_2 &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{750E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
\implies M_2 &= \frac{2}{3}\left(\log\left(750\right) + \log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Earthquake Magnitude

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 7:** The Moment-Magnitude Scale (MMS) gives earthquake magnitude as $\displaystyle{M = \frac{2}{3}\log\!\left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)}$, where $E$ is energy released (joules) and $E_0 = 10^{4.4}$ J.

$\left(a\right)$ Find the magnitude of a quake releasing $1.4 \times 10^{13}$ J.

$\left(b\right)$ A second earthquake has 750 times the energy of a magnitude-3.9 quake. Find its magnitude.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)**

\begin{align} M &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{1.4 \times 10^{13}}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(1.4\times 10^{8.6}\right)\\
&\approx 5.83
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(b)** Let $E_1$ be the energy released by the $3.9$ magnitude earthquake.

\begin{align} M_2 &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{750E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
\implies M_2 &= \frac{2}{3}\left(\log\left(750\right) + \log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\right)\\
\implies M_2 &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(750\right) + \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Earthquake Magnitude

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 7:** The Moment-Magnitude Scale (MMS) gives earthquake magnitude as $\displaystyle{M = \frac{2}{3}\log\!\left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)}$, where $E$ is energy released (joules) and $E_0 = 10^{4.4}$ J.

$\left(a\right)$ Find the magnitude of a quake releasing $1.4 \times 10^{13}$ J.

$\left(b\right)$ A second earthquake has 750 times the energy of a magnitude-3.9 quake. Find its magnitude.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)**

\begin{align} M &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{1.4 \times 10^{13}}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(1.4\times 10^{8.6}\right)\\
&\approx 5.83
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(b)** Let $E_1$ be the energy released by the $3.9$ magnitude earthquake.

\begin{align} M_2 &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{750E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\left(\log\left(750\right) + \log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(750\right) + \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&\approx 1.917 + \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Earthquake Magnitude

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 7:** The Moment-Magnitude Scale (MMS) gives earthquake magnitude as $\displaystyle{M = \frac{2}{3}\log\!\left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)}$, where $E$ is energy released (joules) and $E_0 = 10^{4.4}$ J.

$\left(a\right)$ Find the magnitude of a quake releasing $1.4 \times 10^{13}$ J.

$\left(b\right)$ A second earthquake has 750 times the energy of a magnitude-3.9 quake. Find its magnitude.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)**

\begin{align} M &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{1.4 \times 10^{13}}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(1.4\times 10^{8.6}\right)\\
&\approx 5.83
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(b)** Let $E_1$ be the energy released by the $3.9$ magnitude earthquake.

\begin{align} M_2 &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{750E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\left(\log\left(750\right) + \log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(750\right) + \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&\approx 1.917 + \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= 1.917 + 3.9
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Application to Earthquake Magnitude

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 7:** The Moment-Magnitude Scale (MMS) gives earthquake magnitude as $\displaystyle{M = \frac{2}{3}\log\!\left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)}$, where $E$ is energy released (joules) and $E_0 = 10^{4.4}$ J.

$\left(a\right)$ Find the magnitude of a quake releasing $1.4 \times 10^{13}$ J.

$\left(b\right)$ A second earthquake has 750 times the energy of a magnitude-3.9 quake. Find its magnitude.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(a)**

\begin{align} M &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{1.4 \times 10^{13}}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(1.4\times 10^{8.6}\right)\\
&\approx 5.83
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**(b)** Let $E_1$ be the energy released by the $3.9$ magnitude earthquake.

\begin{align} M_2 &= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{750E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\left(\log\left(750\right) + \log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\right)\\
&= \frac{2}{3}\log\left(750\right) + \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&\approx 1.917 + \frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{E_1}{10^{4.4}}\right)\\
&= 1.917 + 3.9\\
&\approx 5.82
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Applications Practice

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Try It! 5:** A bacterial culture starts with 500 cells and grows continuously at a rate of 8% per hour. How many hours does it take for the culture to reach 2{,}000 cells? Leave your answer in exact form, then approximate to the nearest tenth of an hour.

<br/>
<br/>
<br/>
<br/>

**Try It! 6:** Two earthquakes occur on the same day. The first has magnitude 4.5 on the MMS scale. The second releases $\frac{1}{200}$ of the energy of the first. Find the magnitude of the second earthquake. Round to the nearest hundredth.

</div>

## Exit Ticket Task

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="75%"}

Navigate to [our MAT142 Exit Ticket Form](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSeUo4K9WBH8xFAVjt3VbJAtkSMimu9DGD7Jv6zPLhZhifCIfQ/viewform?usp=dialog), answer the questions, and complete the *task* below.

<br/>

*Note.* Today's discussion is listed as `18. Logarithmic Functions and Equations`

:::

:::{.column width="25%"}

![](MAT142_ExitTicket_QR.png)

:::

::::

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Task:** Consider $h\left(x\right) = \ln\!\left(x + 3\right) - 2$.

$\left(a\right)$ State the domain and vertical asymptote.

$\left(b\right)$ Find the $x$-intercept.

$\left(c\right)$ Find $h^{-1}\!\left(x\right)$ and state its domain.

</div>

## Summary and Next Time...

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.nonincremental}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

<center>**Ideas From Today**</center>

+ $f\left(x\right) = \log_a\!\left(x\right)$ is the inverse of $a^x$. Every feature swaps: domain becomes range, $y$-intercept becomes $x$-intercept, horizontal asymptote becomes vertical.
+ Transformations shift the vertical asymptote and domain, change intercepts, and alter end behavior.
+ The general exponential growth/decay model is $A = Pe^{rt}$. Solving for $t$ always requires a logarithm.
+ Logarithmic scales (like MMS) compress enormous ranges of values into manageable numbers — a difference of 1 on the scale corresponds to a factor of $10^{3/2} \approx 31.6$ in energy.

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

<center>**Looking Ahead**</center>

+ Our next stop is **trigonometry** — a genuinely new area of mathematics covering angles, circular functions, and periodic behavior.
+ If you deal with angles, objects in motion, items rotating, etc. then you need trigonometry.
+ This is the last major topic of the course, but we'll spend several class meetings on it.

:::

::::

:::

:::{.fragment}

<center>**Next Time:** <br/> Introduction to Trigonometry</center>

:::

:::{.fragment}

<center>**Homework:** <br/> Complete Homework 11 on MyOpenMath</center>

:::

</div>
